critique.summary: The article criticizes Microsoft's handling of Windows 11, highlighting forced AI integration, ads in the OS, mandatory Microsoft accounts, OneDrive auto-sync issues, privacy concerns, and hardware obsolescence. It argues that Microsoft's recent 7-point fix plan addresses only superficial UI annoyances while ignoring deeper privacy and control problems.
mainPoints: Microsoft forcibly integrated Copilot AI into many Windows 11 apps starting in September 2023, with unremovable buttons and shortcuts.
Update KB5036980 in April 2024 introduced ads into the Start menu, lock screen, Settings, File Explorer, and notifications.
By October 2025, Microsoft eliminated all workarounds for creating local accounts, requiring Microsoft accounts tied to the cloud from first boot.
OneDrive auto-sync was enabled without user consent, moving files to the cloud and deleting local copies when storage limits are exceeded.
The Windows Recall feature took frequent screenshots stored in plaintext, exposing sensitive data to potential malware.
Windows 11 hardware requirements rendered roughly 20% of PCs obsolete, forcing many users to upgrade or pay for extended Windows 10 support.
Microsoft Edge uses dark patterns to promote usage but maintains only about 5.35% global market share despite these tactics.
Telemetry cannot be fully disabled on Windows 11 Home and Pro editions; only Enterprise editions allow this.
The EU fined Microsoft over €2.2 billion for antitrust violations, including €561 million for breaking a browser ballot promise.
Microsoft's announced fix plan focuses on UI annoyances but excludes major issues like telemetry, forced accounts, OneDrive behavior, and data collection.
critique.truthfulness: Most factual claims in the article are supported by publicly available information and credible sources. The timeline of Copilot integration, the introduction of ads via update KB5036980, the enforcement of Microsoft accounts, OneDrive auto-sync controversies, and telemetry limitations are well-documented in tech news and official Microsoft communications. EU antitrust fines and hardware requirements for Windows 11 are also verifiable. However, some characterizations use strong language and framing that reflect the author's critical opinion. The article does not cite multiple sources or present Microsoft's perspectives, limiting its journalistic balance.
considerations: Microsoft's rationale for integrating AI and ads may include business model sustainability and feature innovation.
The security improvements and user benefits of Windows 11 features like Recall and OneDrive auto-sync are not discussed.
The article does not include Microsoft's official responses or plans beyond the announced 7-point fix.
The impact of hardware requirements on security and performance is not explored.
User choice and opt-in/opt-out options for features like telemetry and AI integration may vary by edition and region.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on dissatisfaction with Microsoft's recent Windows 11 updates, perceived user-hostile practices, and comparisons to past corporate abuses. Many commenters advocate for alternatives like Linux and macOS, criticizing Microsoft's approach to user experience and privacy.

comments.author: Macha
comments.text:
It’s quite common for companies to work their way up to the line of the most user hostile version of their product that users will tolerate. Especially with software where they can just go flip a switch and turn off whatever feature did cross the line but keep everything they gained by inching up to the line, which seems to inevitably result in things like the condition of windows 11.

I think the only way this gets better for consumers is if customer response more often insisted further roll backs than just the last straw if a company crosses the line. The risk of losing other gains at the expense of the user should discourage companies from trying to go full on maximum extraction.

Sadly the only recent cases to achieve that level of success were the reactions to Unity’s install pricing and wizards new OGL. Mostly companies get away with “oh my bad, this final step was just an experiment, we’ve rolled it back for now” to try again later, or just toughing out the negative reception and hoping their competitors come along for the ride too so users have no choice
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced view of corporate behavior, highlighting a pattern of incremental user-hostile changes, which aligns with observed industry tactics.

comments.author: runjake
comments.text:
One point that wasn't mentioned is that we've been through this cycle many times before with Microsoft, and we will again.

It wasn't too long ago that Microsoft went "all in" on Windows for developers and power users. WSL was drastically improved, developer tools were revamped and open sourced. The press adored the "new" Microsoft. Many developers moved over to Windows because they "got the best of both worlds".

And then, they just went back to the same old shit. The thing is, the shit phase of this cycle lasts longer than the non-shit phases.

This next phase is just words, so far. But, mind you, even if Microsoft does produce releases to back up those words, they'll be back to their same old shit within a year or two, once again.

And the whole process will repeat.

PS: And regardless of this supposed change of direction, if you listen to recent statements from Satya and other senior leadership, they are still spewing the same platitudes about agentic computing and software. So what's really going to change long-term?
comments.analysis: The comment accurately describes a recurring pattern in Microsoft's product development, supported by historical examples.

comments.author: ccppurcell
comments.text: I believe they are abusing their customers but I think it's in poor taste to compare this to domestic violence.
comments.analysis: While expressing a strong opinion on abuse, the comparison to domestic violence is inappropriate and detracts from the core critique.

comments.author: ptero
comments.text:
Microsoft lost its way much earlier than 4 years ago. It abused users at the time of Netscape wars and forcing Internet Explorer down people's throats.

But they hit an infinite gold mine with government adoption and for the last 30 years no amount of bad engineering was able to shake off government use.

Windows 11 is bad? Yes, but did you try Microsoft Teams? The only way to force Microsoft into "users matter" engineering is to get govvies off it. My 2c.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly points out Microsoft's reliance on government contracts, which has historically insulated it from user dissatisfaction.

comments.author: neves
comments.text:
For me the worst is Microsoft Start news. I've already turned it off multiple times and it always come back.

To add insult to injury, it always displays terrible gossip, sports or far right news.

If any developer that works in MS news service is reading this message, please know that I hate you.
comments.analysis: The comment is subjective and based on personal experience; it does not provide verifiable evidence of systemic issues.

comments.author: smukherjee19
comments.text: Just a guess, with Macbook Neo releasing, M$ likely got scared of mass exodus to MacOS (the average PC user can now consider a Macbook and it likely wipes the floor with all Windows laptops out there) and decided to throw in a damage control piece.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a realistic perspective on corporate accountability, acknowledging progress while recognizing ongoing issues.

comments.author: Havoc
comments.text:
Their office subscriptions are also going up in price at. Crazy rates. Giving Stiff competition to food price inflation

All because it has some AI stuff on it that I don’t want.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately describes a common complaint about rising subscription costs linked to new features.

comments.author: whatsupdog
comments.text: Don't care about windows. Haven't used a windows computer in over 20 years. Happy Ubuntu user here. What bothers me is the upcoming Android restrictions. I distribute an app that none of the app stores want to touch with a 10 foot pole. That's fine -their store, their choice. But now, to distribute the app from my website I have to jump through hoops and pay their stupid fees through a credit card (at a time when I'm trying to stay anonymous because of the nature of the app). I don't know what to do.
comments.analysis: The comment combines personal preference with a valid concern about platform restrictions impacting developers.

comments.author: 1vuio0pswjnm7
comments.text:
Love the quiz at the end

It's remarkable that computer users are paying $139 to give data to Microsoft through an ad-supported "operating system"

Back in the day (generally) only OEMs paid

What is the $139 for
comments.analysis: This is a rhetorical question without concrete evidence; the cost is generally associated with licensing and services, not just data.

comments.author: g947o
comments.text:
I had enough of Windows 11's ads that I bought a Mac mini for personal use and requested a Macbook to replace my Windows laptop.

I will have to use Teams and Outlook at work because I don't have a choice. But that's it Microsoft.
comments.analysis: Personal anecdote that reflects broader dissatisfaction, supported by known issues with Windows 11.
